_meta.review_status: PENDING — analyst to review via HTML report

page_1.title: Industry Overview — Independent Tower Model
page_1.subtitle: Risk is rising in SSA towers: MNO bargaining power and high capex requirements are the primary structural risks.

key_risks: Large MNOs (Airtel, MTN, Vodacom, Orange, Millicom) hold strong negotiating leverage; 98.4% of revenue from multinational MNOs
SLA and network uptime obligations expose operators to contract penalties
Low differentiation — passive infrastructure largely standardised across providers
MNO consolidation in select markets could reduce tenancy ratios and contracted revenue
cf_impact: Pricing concessions and SLA-driven costs compress EBITDA margins
Risk of DSCR deterioration if anchor tenants renegotiate or exit
Sales shortfall on below-plan tenancy growth
csf: Scale — 14,746 towers; #1 in 7 of 9 markets increases MNO dependency and bargaining leverage
Long-term CPI-linked contracts (avg. 6.6 yrs; US$5.31bn contracted revenue) lock in sales visibility
High network uptime / SLA performance as competitive differentiator
Disciplined pricing on acquisitions to preserve return thresholds

risks.theme: High Capital Requirements & Growth Execution Risk
key_risks: Significant capex required for build-to-suit programme — growth capex US$109.6m (61.1% of total FY25 capex)
Competition for quality tower assets drives up acquisition multiples
Integration and operational execution risk post-acquisition
Net leverage 3.4x at year-end — at top of management comfort range (2.5–3.5x)
cf_impact: Growth capex persistently exceeds depreciation → sustained DSCR compression
Risk of overpaying for acquisitions → impairment charges / negative FCF
Leverage remains elevated until organic tenancy growth accelerates cash flow
Minority buyout liabilities (US$19.3m, up 5x YoY) represent off-B/S obligation
csf: Disciplined build programme management — maintenance capex ring-fenced at US$41.2m
Rigorous acquisition due diligence — return hurdles and tenancy underwrite
Maintain access to debt and equity capital markets
Prioritise deleveraging over M&A — leverage improved 4.2x → 3.4x YoY

page_2.title: Industry Overview — Independent Tower Model
page_2.subtitle: FX volatility and energy/political risk compound the structural pressures, particularly in Sub-Saharan Africa.

key_risks: SSA currencies (TZS, GHS, CDF, ZAR) subject to material devaluation risk; 29% of revenue from non-hard-currency markets
Tower contracts often USD-linked but sales partially collected in local FX
Multi-country inflation and interest rate divergence across markets
IAS 29 hyperinflationary accounting: DRC and Congo B remain in scope post Ghana removal (Jul 2025)
cf_impact: USD-reporting shortfall when local currencies depreciate
FCF available for USD debt service reduced by FX translation losses
Higher effective cost of local currency working capital
Finance cost FX line swung US$40m between FY24 and FY25 [V]
csf: Geographic diversification across 9 markets limits single-currency FX concentration
USD-linked lease escalators partially offset FX depreciation [V]
Partial hedging in place for USD vs. key local currencies
Energy transition reduces diesel cost exposure to FX volatility

strengths: 9-market geographic spread limits single-currency FX concentration; USD-linked escalators and partial hedging in place [V]
71% of Adj EBITDA from hard-currency markets (4 of 9) provides structural USD anchor
weaknesses: DRC (CDF), Ghana (GHS) and Tanzania (TZS) devaluations suppressed USD sales growth FY2023/24 [V]
USD debt vs. local-currency collections creates structural FX mismatch; 29% soft-currency revenue exposure

key_risks: Diesel dependency (~20–25% of opex) exposes margins to oil price swings
Political instability in DRC, Congo Brazzaville and other markets
Regulatory risk: tower sharing mandates, spectrum policy, tax regimes
ESG pressure: rising Scope 1 scrutiny on diesel generators; US$71.7m unresolved DRC tax contingency
cf_impact: EBITDA margin compression from fuel price surges
Site disruption, confiscation or contract termination in high-risk markets
ESG non-compliance costs and reputational risk
UTP crystallisation could be material — no quantum disclosed; #1 audit KAM FY2025
csf: Solar-hybrid and battery storage rollout reduces diesel opex and Scope 1 exposure
Energy cost pass-through in customer contracts
Geographic spread across 9 markets limits political concentration
Proactive tax management with external specialists across all jurisdictions

strengths: Solar-hybrid deployment underway; energy cost pass-through in MNO contracts; 9-market spread limits political concentration risk
weaknesses: Diesel still ~20–25% of opex; DRC and Congo Brazzaville carry high political risk; South Africa competitive headwinds; rising Scope 1 scrutiny [V]
US$71.7m unresolved DRC tax contingency; UTPs = #1 key audit matter FY2025
